result.markdown: --- author: jshn9515 date: 2026-05-05 date-modified: 2026-05-05 open-graph: title: ch8.5-positional-encoding description: "8.7 Transformer Decoder: Masked Self-Attention and Cross-Attention" title: "8.7 Transformer Decoder: Masked Self-Attention and Cross-Attention" --- Earlier, we looked at the Transformer Encoder. The core task of the Encoder is to represent each token in the input sequence as a vector with contextual information. After multiple layers of self-attention and feed-forward network, each position in the input sequence can already fuse information from other positions. If we are only doing tasks like text classification, sequence labeling, or image classification, an Encoder is often enough. That is because these tasks usually only need to understand the input and then make predictions based on the input representation. But if the task is a **sequence-to-sequence generation task**, such as machine translation, text summarization, or image captioning, the situation is different. The model not only has to understand the input, but also has to generate the output step by step. For example, in machine translation, the input is an English sentence: ```text I love deep learning. ``` The output may be a Chinese sentence: ```text 我喜欢深度学习。 ``` At this point, the model needs to do two things: 1. First understand the source-language sentence; 2. Then, based on the target words that have already been generated, continue generating the next target word. The Encoder is responsible for the first thing, and the Decoder is responsible for the second thing. Therefore, the Transformer Decoder can be understood as a Transformer on the generation side. It not only has to look at what it has already generated, but also has to read source-sequence information from the Encoder output. ::: {#fecc2e14 .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} import torch import torch.nn as nn from torch import Tensor print('PyTorch version:', torch.__version__) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` PyTorch version: 2.11.0+xpu ``` ::: ::: ## 8.7.1 What Is Different Between the Decoder and the Encoder Structurally, the Transformer Decoder is very similar to the Encoder. Both are formed by stacking multiple blocks. Each block also contains attention, residual connections, LayerNorm, and a feed-forward network. However, compared with the Encoder, the Decoder has two extra key points. The first difference is that self-attention in the Decoder needs a mask. In generation tasks, the model generates tokens from left to right, step by step. When it predicts the $t$-th token, it can only see the tokens that have already been generated before it, and it cannot peek at future tokens. Otherwise, the model would not be predicting the next word; it would be copying the answer. Therefore, self-attention in the Decoder must use a causal mask, so that each position can only attend to itself and previous positions, and cannot see future positions. The second difference is that the Decoder also needs cross-attention. The Decoder cannot only look at the part it has already generated. It also needs to refer to the Encoder's understanding of the input sequence. For example, during translation, when generating a Chinese word, it needs to repeatedly look back at which parts of the English source sentence are most relevant. This process is done through cross-attention. So, a standard Transformer Decoder Block usually contains three parts: 1. Masked Self-Attention; 2. Cross-Attention; 3. Position-wise Feed-Forward Network. ## 8.7.2 Masked Self-Attention: Preventing the Decoder from Peeking at the Future Let's first look at self-attention inside the Decoder. In the Encoder, each position can see the entire input sequence. For example, if the input sentence has 5 tokens, then the 1st token can attend to the 2nd, 3rd, 4th, and 5th tokens, and the 5th token can also attend to all previous tokens. This is reasonable for understanding tasks. Because the input has already been fully given, the model can of course use context in both directions. But the Decoder is different. The Decoder is used to generate sequences. When generating the first word, the later words have not been generated yet; when generating the second word, it only knows the first word; when generating the third word, it only knows the first two words. In other words, when generating the position $t$, the model can only rely on information from positions 1 to $t$, and cannot rely on information after $t+1$. If we do not add this restriction during training, self-attention will allow every position to see the whole target sequence. For example, when training translation, the target sentence is: ```text 我喜欢深度学习。 ``` When the model predicts “喜欢”, if it can already see the later “深度学习”, isn't that directly peeking at the answer? This is clearly unreasonable. Also, if the model uses future information during training, then during real generation, those future tokens do not exist at all. This will cause the model's performance during inference to drop significantly. Therefore, self-attention in the Decoder must use a **causal mask**, also called a **look-ahead mask**. Its role is: > **Each position can only attend to itself and positions before itself, and cannot attend to future positions.** So, how is it implemented? Assume our target sequence length is 4. Ordinary self-attention computes a $4 \times 4$ attention score matrix: $$ S = \frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} $$ Here, the $i$-th row represents the attention scores from the $i$-th position to all positions. Without a mask, every row can see all columns: $$ \begin{array}{c|cccc} & \text{pos 1} & \text{pos 2} & \text{pos 3} & \text{pos 4} \\ \hline \text{pos 1} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\ \text{pos 2} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\ \text{pos 3} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\ \text{pos 4} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\ \end{array} $$ But in the Decoder, we want it to become: $$ \begin{array}{c|cccc} & \text{pos 1} & \text{pos 2} & \text{pos 3} & \text{pos 4} \\ \hline \text{pos 1} & \checkmark & \times & \times & \times \\ \text{pos 2} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \times & \times \\ \text{pos 3} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \times \\ \text{pos 4} & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark & \checkmark \\ \end{array} $$ That is, the 1st position can only see the 1st position; the 2nd position can see the 1st and 2nd positions; the 3rd position can see the 1st, 2nd, and 3rd positions, and so on. In implementation, we usually set the attention scores of future positions to a very small number, such as $-\infty$: $$ S_{ij} = -\infty, \quad \text{if}\, j > i $$ Then we apply softmax. Because: $$ \exp(-\infty) = 0 $$ these future positions will have attention weights of 0. In this way, the model cannot take information from future tokens. So, masked self-attention can be written as: $$ \operatorname{MaskedAttention}(Q, K, V) = \operatorname{softmax} \left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} + M \right)V $$ where $M$ is the mask matrix. For positions that are allowed to be attended to, $M_{ij}=0$; for positions that are not allowed to be attended to, $M_{ij}=-\infty$. As for why self-attention is needed, it is actually easy to understand. In machine translation, we need to continue generating the next word based on the target words that have already been generated. During this process, we need to know the internal dependencies of the target language. For example, the model may need to determine what the subject is, what the predicate is, and where the current sentence structure has developed. This part of the information does not come from the source sentence, but from the target sentence itself. So Decoder self-attention solves this problem: > **For the current position to be generated, how should it use the already generated target-side context?** It is almost the same as Encoder self-attention in formula. The only difference is the mask. For the target sequence representation $Y$, we have: $$ Q = YW_Q, \quad K = YW_K, \quad V = YW_V $$ Then compute masked self-attention: $$ H = \operatorname{MaskedAttention}(Q, K, V) $$ Here, $Q$, $K$, and $V$ all come from the Decoder's current target sequence representation, so it is still self-attention. ## 8.7.3 Cross-Attention: Letting the Decoder Read the Encoder Output However, masked self-attention alone is not enough. If the Decoder only looks at the tokens it has already generated, it is like an ordinary language model. It can only continue writing based on the previous text. But tasks like machine translation and summarization are not free-form continuation. They need to generate output based on the input content. Therefore, the Decoder must also be able to dynamically read the Encoder output. This step is **cross-attention**. In cross-attention, we have: $$ Q = H_{dec} W_Q, \quad K = H_{enc} W_K, \quad V = H_{enc} W_V $$ Then compute: $$ \operatorname{CrossAttention}(Q, K, V) = \operatorname{softmax} \left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}} \right)V $$ Here, $H_{dec}$ is the representation obtained by the Decoder after masked self-attention, and $H_{enc}$ is the representation obtained after the Encoder encodes the input sequence. Intuitively, the Decoder is using its current state to ask a question: > **What information do I need to find from the source sentence in order to generate the next target word now?** Cross-attention computes the relevance between each Decoder position and each Encoder position, and then retrieves weighted information from the Encoder's values. This is why cross-attention connects the Encoder and the Decoder. If you still remember Bahdanau attention from earlier, you will find that cross-attention is very similar to early attention. In early Bahdanau attention, every time the Decoder generates a word, it takes the current decoder hidden state and matches it with all encoder hidden states. Then it computes a weighted sum of the encoder hidden states to get a context vector. Mapped to the Transformer: - The Decoder hidden state is like the query; - The Encoder hidden state is like the key and value; - The context vector obtained by weighted summation is like the cross-attention output. So, cross-attention in the Transformer Decoder is not a completely unfamiliar new thing. It can be seen as the modern version of early encoder-decoder attention inside the Transformer structure. The difference is that Transformer no longer relies on an RNN to generate hidden states step by step. Instead, it uses self-attention and positional encoding to model sequence representations. At the same time, cross-attention is also written in the standard Q, K, V form, and it can be computed in parallel through multi-head attention. ## 8.7.4 Overall Structure of the Decoder Block Now we can put together a Transformer Decoder Block. Assume the target sequence representation input to the Decoder Block is $Y$, and the Encoder output is $H_{enc}$. First, perform masked self-attention: $$ Y' = \operatorname{MaskedSelfAttention}(Y) $$ Then add the residual connection and LayerNorm: $$ Y = \operatorname{LayerNorm}(Y + Y') $$ Second, perform cross-attention. At this point, the query comes from the Decoder, while the key and value come from the Encoder: $$ Y' = \operatorname{CrossAttention}(Y, H_{enc}, H_{enc}) $$ Then add the residual connection and LayerNorm again: $$ Y = \operatorname{LayerNorm}(Y + Y') $$ Third, pass through the position-wise feed-forward network: $$ Y' = \operatorname{FFN}(Y) $$ Finally, add the residual connection and LayerNorm again: $$ Y = \operatorname{LayerNorm}(Y + Y') $$ Therefore, a Decoder Block can be written as: Compared with the Encoder Block, the Decoder Block has one additional cross-attention module, and its self-attention needs a causal mask. ## 8.7.5 PyTorch Implementation of the Transformer Decoder Block Below, we implement a simplified Transformer Decoder Block. To make the structure clearer, we directly use PyTorch's built-in `nn.MultiheadAttention`. ::: {#cca078ed .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} class TransformerDecoderLayer(nn.Module): def __init__( self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, dim_feedforward: int | None = None, dropout: float = 0.1, ): super().__init__() self.d_model = d_model self.num_heads = num_heads if dim_feedforward is None: dim_feedforward = 4 * d_model self.d_ff = dim_feedforward self.self_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention( embed_dim=d_model, num_heads=num_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True, ) self.cross_attn = nn.MultiheadAttention( embed_dim=d_model, num_heads=num_heads, dropout=dropout, batch_first=True, ) self.ffn = nn.Sequential( nn.Linear(d_model, self.d_ff), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(dropout), nn.Linear(self.d_ff, d_model), ) self.norm1 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model) self.norm2 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model) self.norm3 = nn.LayerNorm(d_model) self.dropout1 = nn.Dropout(dropout) self.dropout2 = nn.Dropout(dropout) self.dropout3 = nn.Dropout(dropout) def forward( self, tgt: Tensor, memory: Tensor, tgt_mask: Tensor | None = None, tgt_key_padding_mask: Tensor | None = None, memory_key_padding_mask: Tensor | None = None, ) -> Tensor: self_attn_out, _ = self.self_attn( query=tgt, key=tgt, value=tgt, attn_mask=tgt_mask, key_padding_mask=tgt_key_padding_mask, need_weights=False, ) tgt = self.norm1(tgt + self.dropout1(self_attn_out)) cross_attn_out, _ = self.cross_attn( query=tgt, key=memory, value=memory, key_padding_mask=memory_key_padding_mask, need_weights=False, ) tgt = self.norm2(tgt + self.dropout2(cross_attn_out)) ffn_out = self.ffn(tgt) tgt = self.norm3(tgt + self.dropout3(ffn_out)) return tgt x = torch.randn(2, 16, 512) # (batch_size, seq_len, d_model) memory = torch.randn(2, 32, 512) # (batch_size, src_seq_len, d_model) decoder_layer = TransformerDecoderLayer(d_model=512, num_heads=8) with torch.inference_mode(): output = decoder_layer(x, memory) print('Decoder Block output shape:', output.shape) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Decoder Block output shape: torch.Size([2, 16, 512]) ``` ::: ::: Here, `tgt` is the target sequence representation, which is the sequence currently processed by the Decoder; `memory` is the Encoder output, which is the context representation obtained after the source sequence passes through the Encoder. `tgt_key_padding_mask` and `memory_key_padding_mask` are the padding masks for the target sequence and source sequence respectively. In masked self-attention: ```text target -> query target -> key target -> value ``` In cross-attention: ```text target -> query encoder output -> key encoder output -> value ``` This exactly corresponds to what we discussed earlier: the Decoder uses its own state as the query to retrieve relevant information from the Encoder output. ## 8.7.6 Stacking Decoder Blocks Just like the Encoder, we usually stack multiple Decoder Blocks to form a complete Transformer Decoder. ::: {#9b486a0c .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} nn.Transformer class TransformerDecoder(nn.Module): def __init__( self, d_model: int, num_heads: int, num_layers: int, dim_feedforward: int | None = None, dropout: float = 0.1, ): super().__init__() self.layers = nn.ModuleList( [ TransformerDecoderLayer( d_model=d_model, num_heads=num_heads, dim_feedforward=dim_feedforward, dropout=dropout, ) for _ in range(num_layers) ] ) self.norm = nn.LayerNorm(d_model) def forward( self, tgt: Tensor, memory: Tensor, tgt_mask: Tensor | None = None, tgt_key_padding_mask: Tensor | None = None, memory_key_padding_mask: Tensor | None = None, ) -> Tensor: x = tgt for layer in self.layers: x = layer( x, memory, tgt_mask=tgt_mask, tgt_key_padding_mask=tgt_key_padding_mask, memory_key_padding_mask=memory_key_padding_mask, ) x = self.norm(x) return x x = torch.randn(2, 16, 512) # (batch_size, tgt_seq_len, d_model) memory = torch.randn(2, 32, 512) # (batch_size, src_seq_len, d_model) decoder = TransformerDecoder(d_model=512, num_heads=8, num_layers=6) with torch.inference_mode(): output = decoder(x, memory) print('Decoder output shape:', output.shape) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Decoder output shape: torch.Size([2, 16, 512]) ``` ::: ::: One thing to note is that the Decoder input `tgt` is the representation of the target sequence, not the raw token ids. Usually, we first convert the target token ids into vector representations through an embedding layer, and then feed them into the Decoder. This is the same as how the Encoder input is processed. ## 8.7.7 Generating the Causal Mask After discussing the Decoder structure, let's look at how to generate the causal mask. Below is a simple causal mask generation function: ::: {#9f3e78b2 .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} from torch.types import Device def generate_causal_mask(seq_len: int, device: Device = None) -> Tensor: mask = torch.full((seq_len, seq_len), -torch.inf, device=device) mask = mask.triu(diagonal=1) return mask mask = generate_causal_mask(4) print(mask) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` tensor([[0., -inf, -inf, -inf], [0., 0., -inf, -inf], [0., 0., 0., -inf], [0., 0., 0., 0.]]) ``` ::: ::: It will be added to the attention scores, making the softmax weights at the corresponding positions become 0. Below is a simple example showing how to apply the causal mask to the Transformer Decoder Block: ::: {#913d0728 .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} x = torch.randn(2, 16, 512) # (batch_size, tgt_seq_len, d_model) memory = torch.randn(2, 32, 512) # (batch_size, src_seq_len, d_model) decoder_layer = TransformerDecoderLayer(d_model=512, num_heads=8) mask = generate_causal_mask(x.size(-2), device=x.device) with torch.inference_mode(): output = decoder_layer(x, memory, tgt_mask=mask) print('Decoder Block output shape:', output.shape) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Decoder Block output shape: torch.Size([2, 16, 512]) ``` ::: ::: You can see that we pass the generated causal mask to the `tgt_mask` parameter. In this way, the mask will be automatically applied inside masked self-attention, ensuring that each position can only see itself and previous positions. ## 8.7.8 How the Decoder Is Used for Generation At this point, the core mechanism of the Decoder is basically complete. It first uses masked self-attention to process the target sequence that has already been generated, and then reads source-sequence information from the encoder output through cross-attention. Among these, the causal mask is very important, because it prevents the Decoder from seeing future tokens in advance even during training. Next, we can look at a more practical question: how exactly is the Decoder used to generate text? During training, we usually already have the complete target sequence. For example, the target sentence is: ```text 我 喜欢 深度 学习 ``` What the model needs to predict is: ```text 我 喜欢 深度 学习 ``` Although the complete target sequence is visible during training, because of the causal mask, each position can still only see positions before itself. Therefore, the model will not peek at future answers, and can only predict the next word based on the already generated part. During inference, the Decoder starts from a start token and generates step by step: ```text ``` First, it predicts the first token: ```text 我 ``` Then it appends it back to the input: ```text 我 ``` Then it predicts the next token: ```text 喜欢 ``` This process repeats until `` is generated or the maximum length is reached. So, the generation process of the Decoder is essentially autoregressive: $$ p(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_T \mid x) = \prod_{t=1}^{T} p(y_t \mid y_{
